channel.title: Master Feed (Updated every 30 mins)
channel.description: Master Feed (Updated every 30 mins) generated by script

item.title: U.S. Floats Punishing NATO Members for Refusing to Join Iran War
item.description: An internal Pentagon email suggests suspending Spain from the alliance and reviewing Britain’s claim to the Falkland Islands.

item.title: What in the World?
item.description: Test yourself on the week of April 18: Taiwan’s president faces travel troubles, U.N. secretary-general candidates assemble in New York, and Pope Leo concludes his tour of Africa.

item.title: The Planet Is Doing Better Than You Think
item.description: Apocalyptic headlines overlook conservation and biodiversity successes.

item.title: A mogul alleges he has been swindled by a Trump-affiliated crypto project
item.description: Tom Wolfe could not have invented a more outlandish satire of America in the 2020s

item.title: Hoaxes Keep Escaping Containment
item.description: A delusional U.S. president is helping thin the line between fiction and reality.

item.title: How Gold and Regional Rivalries Drive the War in Sudan
item.description: The world’s worst humanitarian crisis enters its fourth year.

item.title: The Staged Death of China’s Military-Civil Fusion
item.description: Military-civil fusion became a major irritant in China-U.S. relations, so Beijing stopped talking about it. But it didn't stop pursuing the strategy.

item.title: Can Africa Follow Asia’s Development Model?
item.description: An economics writer known for his work on Asia turns his eye toward the fastest-growing continent.

item.title: Decolonizing ‘Moby-Dick’
item.description: Literature’s obsessive quest for anti-canonical adaptations pursues the white whale at its peril.

item.title: Cover Story newsletter: America prepares for the midterms
item.description: How we chose this week’s image

item.title: Is exercise as effective as treatments for depression and anxiety?
item.description: Some big studies say yes. Many experts have reservations

item.title: The Deeper Forces Shaping Global Trade
item.description: It would be natural to assume that tariffs are the driving force behind recent major shifts in global trade patterns. But while geopolitically-driven trade policies are indeed reconfiguring flows, longer-term shifts in technology and economic development are determining what the world builds and buys.

item.title: Why Are the Saudis Sitting Out the War With Iran?
item.description: A more active approach would help cement Riyadh’s regional clout.

item.title: What China’s New County Reveals About Its Afghanistan Policy
item.description: The administrative change hints at China’s plan for greater integration with Afghanistan – and beyond to Central Asia.

item.title: How to Rebuild Hungarian Democracy
item.description: Peter Magyar must avoid reproducing the very abuses he seeks to dismantle.

item.title: Rules for the Rest of Us
item.description: As the Global South knows well, the alternative to international rules is not freedom, but rather the undisguised power of the strongest. But these economies are far from powerless: they have significant leverage, although wielding it requires collective positions, shared frameworks, and coordinated strategies.

item.title: The Quad Isn’t Dead. It’s Just Moved Out of the Spotlight.
item.description: Yes, high-level summits are on hiatus. But the group continues to function in ways that are less visible, more functional, and potentially more durable.

item.title: Papal Politics, Past and Present
item.description: Despite more colorful medieval precedents, Pope Leo’s clash with Trump reflects the Vatican’s postwar peace advocacy.

item.title: Mongolia Is Redefining Steppe Diplomacy With Kazakhstan
item.description: Mongolian President Khurelsukh Ukhnaa’s recent visit to Kazakhstan forges a new model of Eurasian middle power resilience.

item.title: China Fears the Church, America Fears the Chinese: A Chinese Christian Student Caught in Between
item.description: For some Chinese, studying in the U.S. is a welcome breath of freedom. But they are increasingly met not with open arms but with suspicion.

item.title: How Are Bangladesh’s Parties Allocating Reserved Women’s Seats in Parliament?
item.description: The BNP's nomination list reflects a pointedly inclusive approach, while Jamaat’s selections reveal its alliance with the NCP is here to stay.

item.title: How China, Russia, and North Korea Enable Each Other’s Atrocities
item.description: China enables North Korean forced labor, which in turn fuels Russia's continued invasion of Ukraine.

item.title: The Populist vs. the Pope
item.description: The war of words between Pope Leo XIV and US President Donald Trump has revived the age-old clash between the sacred and the secular. But Trump has severely misjudged the “soft power” of the world’s preeminent religious leader, and attacking a popular pontiff will likely come at a high political cost.

item.title: Helping Iran, China Is a Party in the War
item.description: Several reports in recent weeks have revealed the extent of China’s active support for Iran.

item.title: Latin America’s Turn Atop the U.N.?
item.description: The next secretary-general is likely to hail from the region.

item.title: To Save Democracy, Fight Inequality
item.description: When liberal democracy fails to deliver material well-being, its legitimacy erodes, and the far right fills the void. The answer to the resurgence of authoritarianism is not to patch up a broken system, but to confront its underlying causes and rebuild the economic foundations of democratic life.

item.title: The AI Risk We Need to Focus On
item.description: Owing to all the work that was done following the 2008 financial crisis and bank bailouts, regulators now have the tools to prevent an AI-driven financial crisis. But even if that risk is managed, we still need policymakers to get serious about ensuring that the AI revolution works for everyone.

item.title: Ready or Not, Europe’s Post-American Future Has Arrived
item.description: Although alliances as long-lasting and as tested as NATO do not collapse overnight, there is no longer any doubt that the world's most successful security arrangement is now unraveling. With the window for reversing course having closed, Europeans must accept that they are already on their own.

item.title: The Age of Global Un-Order
item.description: As crises become more complex, less predictable, and increasingly intertwined, the global system is no longer anchored by shared rules and norms. In a world where the very idea of order has collapsed, governments must learn to navigate radical uncertainty rather than chasing lost anchors of stability.

item.title: Jakarta’s Washington Pivot
item.description: The recently signed Major Defense Cooperation Partnership marks the beginning of a new phase in U.S.-Indonesia security relations.

item.title: Former Philippine President Duterte to Stand Trial at ICC After Judges Confirm Charges
item.description: A pre-trial panel said that there were "substantial grounds" to believe that the 81-year-old was guilty of crimes against humanity linked to his violent "war on drugs."

item.title: The Other China Flash Point
item.description: Like Taiwan, the South China Sea could spark a U.S.-China war.

item.title: US Government Sanctions Cambodian Senator Kok An For Involvement in Online Scams
item.description: The announcement was Washington's latest effort to combat Southeast Asia's multibillion-dollar online scamming industry.

item.title: Will Japan-North Korea Relations Change?
item.description: The government of Sanae Takaichi may need to risk its political standing.

item.title: Trump’s Iran War Approaches a Fresh Legal Hurdle
item.description: Pressure will increase on congressional Republicans to vote to end the war if it surpasses the legal time limit.

item.title: An Economic War of Attrition
item.description: The U.S. and Iran have gone from lobbing missiles to inflicting pocketbook pain.

item.title: Lebanon Seeks to Extend Israel-Hezbollah Cease-Fire
item.description: But Hezbollah’s opposition to talks and continued Israeli strikes make progress difficult to come by.

item.title: What West Bengal’s Election Means for Bangladesh
item.description: The state election results stand to impact border enforcement, trade flows, and water negotiations, as well as political messaging on both sides of the border.

item.description: The talks between the neighboring states are about defeating their mutual enemy.

item.title: Iran Is Calling the Shots Now
item.description: Tehran is following Ho Chi Minh’s playbook in Vietnam.

item.title: Kazakhstan Is Slowly Integrating UAVs Into Its Armed Forces
item.description: As drones prove decisive in modern warfare, Kazakhstan is working to embed them in its military while slowly making a shift toward domestic production.

item.description: It’s not the first time that it has toyed with the unwelcome idea

item.description: Trump’s inexplicable effort to lift sanctions on Eritrea reflects a deepening lack of strategy.

item.description: It is hard to imagine the grouping enduring another two and a half years of Donald Trump.

item.description: Reversing vaccine mandates is a disaster for military readiness.

item.title: Nepal’s Remittance Reckoning: The Gen Z Mandate Meets the Gulf Crisis
item.description: Kathmandu's new government was elected to right Nepal’s economic ship. It must now confront an external economic threat.

item.title: Africa Must Turn Climate Targets Into Green Strategies
item.description: African policymakers often take a “wish list” approach to their climate-action plans, without thinking about their investment potential or how they contribute to economic transformation. But a new framework can help them identify the most promising opportunities and match them with the right type of capital.

item.title: Spain’s China Bridge: Diplomacy or Dependency?
item.description: Securing a national win at the expense of a coherent EU strategy would be a self-defeating gamble for Spain.

item.title: After Trump, Partisanship Will Still Undermine U.S. Credibility
item.description: For foreign audiences, the dysfunctional nature of U.S. politics matters more than the country’s leader.

item.title: Breaking the Postwar Taboo: Japan Lifts Its Ban on Lethal Arms Exports
item.description: To fully understand its significance, we must situate the Cabinet decision within both its historical context and the evolving international environment.

item.description: Despite consistent chatter about the need for diversification, Ashgabat has struggled to reach customers other than China given infrastructure constraints.

item.description: Increased diplomatic clout made mediation possible, but the real motivation is the potential devastation the war could cause to Pakistan itself.

item.title: Europe’s Economic Emergency
item.description: Rather than flit from one challenge to another, European leaders must get their priorities straight. Achieving stronger economic growth is essential because a robust industrial and technological base is a prerequisite for self-defense and strategic autonomy in an increasingly dangerous world.

item.title: A Test for Timor-Leste, and ASEAN, on Myanmar
item.description: Timor-Leste’s accession has created a rare opening for ASEAN to clarify whether it stands for anything more than procedural restraint.

item.title: Why Asia Feels the Hormuz Crisis Most
item.description: For Asia, the disruption to energy markets and global trade is immediate and consequential.

item.title: Policymakers vs. the Iran War Shock
item.description: Because markets, central banks, and governments move at different speeds—and sometimes in different directions—mistimed policy responses to major shocks can exacerbate, rather than mitigate, the depth and duration of a crisis. Fortunately, if this major lesson from the 1970s is heeded, the risk can be managed.

item.title: Honking is harming India’s health—and its economy
item.description: But nobody seems to care

item.title: Why Slums Persist
item.description: Slums persist not because they are desirable, but because they are necessary. The task for policymakers is not to eliminate slums by diktat, but rather to make them obsolete over time by promoting the educational opportunities and spatial integration their residents need.

item.description: Too many no longer believe elections are fair

item.description: Whether or not it was serious, Purbaya Yudhi Sadewa's suggestion is a sign of how far the norm of freedom of peaceful navigation has been eroded by the Iran war.

item.title: The Australian Labor Party’s Foreign Policy No Longer Matches the World’s Realities
item.description: The party's platform clings to the U.S. alliance while ignoring its own commitment to self-reliance and the opportunities of the Asia-Pacific.

item.title: Is a Private-Credit Crisis Imminent?
item.description: The rapid expansion of opaque and minimally regulated lending outside the traditional banking system over the past 15 years largely flew—as intended—under regulators’ radar. Now, however, signs of trouble in shadow banking are multiplying, with rising redemptions and notable fund failures stoking fears of a 2008-style crisis. Though we might not be headed toward a meltdown just yet, regulators, especially in the United States, are turning a blind eye to the growing risks.

item.title: Is a Perfect Financial Storm Gathering?
item.description: Between the explosive growth of shadow banking, the relentless rise in public debt across major economies, and stalled implementation of the regulatory reforms enacted after the 2008 financial crisis, those charged with maintaining financial stability have much reason to worry. The window to do something about it is closing fast.

item.title: Morocco’s New Model Economy
item.description: Africa’s future will not be written by countries waiting for perfect conditions. It will be written by countries like Morocco, which made the most of its undesirable post-colonial inheritance to become not only an economic success story but a potential model for others to emulate.

item.title: Does Private-Credit Smoke Mean Financial Fire?
item.description: Does the rapid growth in non-bank lending pose a threat to the stability of the financial system? While the current chair of the US Securities and Exchange Commission insists that it does not, there are growing signs that something is rotten in the state of private credit.

item.description: The selection process for António Guterres’s successor begins with a grilling for four contenders.

item.description: Despite the campaign rhetoric, so far, there are no concrete signs of a rapprochement with Taiwan by Honduras’ new government.

item.title: Will AI Solve Rich Countries’ Debt Woes?
item.description: Although many commentators believe that AI has the potential to supercharge economic growth and solve our most vexing problems, such optimism underplays the risks. If anything, the technology is likely to cause profound—and costly—problems on the way to the halcyon future.

item.title: Why Trump Should Make China-US Relations Great Again
item.description: A country insulates itself from China at its own peril, at least economically. The road to making America great again must go through Beijing.

item.description: Closing Suwung landfill is the right decision. But the government has yet to provide sustainable alternatives for Bali's waste.

item.title: Escaping the IMF Austerity Trap
item.description: There is ample research showing that growth-oriented strategies accompanied by redistribution policies are the most promising path to recovery across the Global South. Rather than imposing cookie-cutter austerity programs, the International Monetary Fund should be following the evidence.

item.title: Decoupling and South Korea’s New Capitalism
item.description: The South Korean stock market’s remarkable recent performance reflects not only successful government initiatives but also the rapid rise of individual investors. Now, instead of always reinvesting profits, Korean firms are delivering more short-term value to shareholders—a change that could help keep capital in the country.

item.title: American Jews’ Netanyahu Problem
item.description: Political polarization, the collapse of institutional gatekeepers, and social media’s amplification of fringe voices were always going to put pressure on American Jews. But Benjamin Netanyahu's alliances and policies have compounded these forces, putting US Jews in the crossfire of partisan politics and Middle East adventurism.

item.title: The Deafening Silence on Offshore Wealth
item.description: The kind of hidden wealth that the Panama Papers exposed a decade ago fuels corruption, distorts markets, drives inequality, finances authoritarian regimes, and weakens democracies. Yet it endures—not because it is inevitable, but because powerful interests benefit from it.

item.description: The PLA is sending military signals amid the Philippines-U.S. Balikatan exercise and ahead of the Trump-Xi summit.

item.description: Amid Tokyo’s arms export shift, one massive potential market still remains untapped.

item.title: The Risky Geography of the Cloud
item.description: Iran's recent drone strikes on Gulf states mark the first time commercial data centers have been targeted in an organized fashion. Europe, especially, should reflect on the assumptions underlying digital-infrastructure investments and deployments, because attacks on data centers in the Middle East can jeopardize its own economic security.

item.title: The Real Reason Taiwan’s Defense Procurement Is Stalling
item.description: Doubts about U.S. credibility are making arms purchases harder to sustain.

item.title: Why Do Uzbek Female Labor Migrants Chose Turkiye?
item.description: Migration enables Uzbek women to renegotiate traditional gender roles and assume greater economic responsibility, even if their experiences are shaped by legal precarity and social vulnerability.

item.title: Trump’s Self-Defeating Attacks on the Fed
item.description: Instead of quietly winding down the meritless investigation of US Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell, the Trump administration has ramped it up, with prosecutors showing up unannounced at the Fed’s construction site. But the president's political charade will only delay the confirmation of Kevin Warsh as Powell’s successor.

item.title: The Strongman Era Has Peaked
item.description: Why a global era of authoritarian governance may be coming to an end.

item.title: The World After Trump
item.description: The international economic order that has prevailed since the end of World War II is gone for good. With the United States abdicating its leadership responsibilities, it falls to middle powers to construct a system that accommodates national preferences while safeguarding global stability.

item.title: Reevaluating ASEAN’s Economic Outlook Amid the Iran Conflict
item.description: Marcus Tantau from Templeton Research offers fresh insights into the region's economies as they reel from the impacts of the Hormuz oil crisis.

item.title: The West Is Still Getting Russia Wrong
item.description: While Vladimir Putin views Russia as a great power and civilizational counterweight to Western liberalism, he does not have a coherent plan to remake the world, let alone the capacity to do so. Because of this weakness, the Kremlin is focused not on domination, but on disruption.

item.title: The Global AI Threat Has Arrived
item.description: The ability of Anthropic’s new AI model, Claude Mythos Preview, to find vulnerabilities across major operating systems and web browsers has dangerous implications for today’s highly interconnected world. Instead of merely securing its own firms, the United States must address the problem through diplomacy, particularly with China.

item.title: ASEAN’s Rules of Origin Need a Rethink
item.description: ATIGA review and industrial strategy in an age of geopolitical risk

item.title: Modi Government Suffers Setback as Constitutional Amendment Defeated in Parliament
item.description: It clubbed an already passed amendment on women’s reservation with the controversial amendment to delimit electoral boundaries. The ploy flopped.

item.title: A Brief History of Southeast Asia’s Streaming Wars

item.title: The Next UN Secretary-General Must Be a Woman

This would not be a corrective gesture or a form of symbolic representation, but rather a strategic acknowledgment that leadership selection must reflect the realities of contemporary governance.
